1: But now hear, O Jacob my servant, Israel whom I have chosen. The adversative but-now pivots from the idol polemic that will dominate vv 9-20 back to the identity of the covenant community: the servant Jacob, the chosen Israel. These titles — servant and chosen — are the same applied to the Servant of the later poems (Isaiah 42:1, Isaiah 49:3, Isaiah 52:13): the nation and the individual Servant are named by the same titles, raising the question of which Israel is the true fulfillment. In the NT, the church as the body of the chosen servant (1 Peter 2:9: a chosen people, a royal priesthood) inherits these titles as YHWH's words to Jacob are applied to the community that participates in the Servant's election.
2: Thus says YHWH who made you, who formed you in the womb, who will help you: Do not fear, O Jacob my servant, Jeshurun whom I have chosen. The forming-in-the-womb language echoes the Servant's calling in Isaiah 49:1 (YHWH called me from the womb; he named me before I was born) and Jeremiah's call (Jeremiah 1:5). Jeshurun is the poetic name for Israel meaning the upright one — used also in Deuteronomy 32:15, Isaiah 33:5, Isaiah 33:26. The making and forming by YHWH establishes that Israel's identity is a divine gift, not a human achievement: the same theological foundation that Paul applies to the election of the new covenant community (Ephesians 2:10: we are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus).
3: For I will pour water on the thirsty land and streams on the dry ground; I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring and my blessing on your descendants. The Spirit-pouring on offspring is one of the great eschatological promises: the same ground that thirsts for water thirsts for the Spirit, and YHWH will satisfy both thirst with the same act of pouring. Joel 2:28-29 elaborates this promise (I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh) which Peter cites at Pentecost (Acts 2:17-18) as fulfilled on the day of the Spirit's outpouring. Jesus combines the images: if anyone is thirsty, let him come to me and drink — rivers of living water will flow from within him, which he said about the Spirit (John 7:37-39). Isaiah 44:3 is the fountainhead of the water-and-Spirit cluster that drives the NT Pentecost theology.
4: They will spring up like grass in a meadow, like poplar trees by flowing streams. The growth of the Spirit-filled offspring is vegetative abundance: grass in a meadow, poplars by water. The poplar-by-water image is the Ps 1 image (like a tree planted by streams of water that bears fruit in season). In the NT, the fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22-23) is the Spirit-poured-out community's natural growth: the Spirit produces what the flesh could not — the sustainable abundance of a tree planted by living water.
5: One will say I belong to YHWH; another will call on the name of Jacob; yet another will write on his hand Belonging to YHWH and will take the name Israel. The voluntary self-inscription belonging to YHWH is the eschatological reversal of the slave-brand: instead of being marked as property of a human master, people choose to mark themselves as belonging to YHWH. In the NT, the seal of the Spirit (Ephesians 1:13-14: having believed, you were marked with a seal, the promised Holy Spirit) and the new name written on a white stone (Revelation 2:17) are this inscription-logic applied to the new covenant community. Revelation 7:3 and 14:1 show the servants of God sealed on their foreheads — the Isaianic marking-of-belonging as the divine protective seal.
6: Thus says YHWH, the King of Israel, and his Redeemer, YHWH of hosts: I am the first and I am the last; apart from me there is no God. The title I am the first and I am the last is the most striking Christological anticipation in Isaiah. The Risen Jesus takes this title directly: Do not be afraid. I am the First and the Last (Revelation 1:17); I am the First and the Last, who died and came to life again (Revelation 2:8); I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End (Revelation 22:13). YHWH's self-declaration of being the eternal bookends of all reality is applied by the glorified Christ to himself — the NT's strongest claim for the divine identity of Jesus within the monotheistic framework of Isaiah 44:6.
7: Who then is like me? Let him proclaim it, declare it, and set it before me. Who has announced from of old the things to come? Let him tell us what is to happen. The challenge-to-the-gods is the prophetic court scene: YHWH alone knows the future and has announced it from of old. Prophecy is the epistemological evidence of YHWH's uniqueness — no other god predicted history in advance. In the NT, the fulfillment of OT prophecy in Jesus is the same epistemological argument: these things happened so that the scripture would be fulfilled (John 19:36); this was to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet (Matthew 1:22, Isaiah 2:15, etc.). The predictive-prophecy argument for YHWH's uniqueness becomes the predictive-prophecy argument for Jesus's identity as YHWH's action in history.
8: Do not tremble, do not be afraid; have I not told you and declared it long ago? You are my witnesses. Is there any God besides me? There is no other Rock — I know of none. The Rock title for YHWH (cf. Deuteronomy 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31; Psalms 18:2) is the foundation-metaphor for divine reliability. Paul applies this to Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:4: they drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ. The Rock that sustained Israel in the wilderness — the divine Presence that provided water from the rock (Exodus 17:6, Numbers 20:11) — is identified by Paul as the pre-incarnate Christ. There is no Rock besides YHWH; Christ is the Rock; therefore Christ participates in YHWH's unique divine identity.
9: Those who fashion an idol are all of them nothing; the things they treasure are worthless. Their witnesses see nothing, know nothing, so that they will be put to shame. The idol-polemic section (vv 9-20) is the most sustained anti-idolatry argument in the OT. The witnesses who see nothing and know nothing are the humans who worship the manufactured objects — the idol-worshiper takes on the non-seeing, non-knowing quality of the idol itself (Psalms 115:8: those who make them will be like them). Paul echoes this in Romans 1:21-22: although they knew God, they neither glorified him as God; their thinking became futile and their foolish hearts were darkened — the worshiper becomes the thing worshiped.
10: Who forms a god and casts an idol that can profit nothing? The rhetorical question exposes the absurdity: who crafts a god? The god is the product of human skill; the skill is itself YHWH's gift. The idol that profits nothing is the manufactured antithesis of the real God who profits everything — the same contrast as 1 Corinthians 8:4 (we know that an idol is nothing at all in the world) and 1 Thessalonians 1:9 (you turned to God from idols, to serve the living and true God). The profit-language is economic: idols are a zero-return investment; YHWH is the source of all genuine benefit.
11: Indeed, all its worshipers will be put to shame; the craftsmen are only human. Let them all come together and take their stand; they will be terrified and put to shame together. The shame of idol-worship is the eschatological verdict: those who trusted manufactured gods will find their trust was misplaced when YHWH's truth becomes undeniable. In the NT, shame is the opposite of justification: everyone who trusts in him will not be put to shame (Romans 10:11, citing Isaiah 28:16). The shame of the idol-worshiper is the verdict that gospel-faith avoids — the same forensic category applied to the new covenant community.
12: The blacksmith takes a tool and works the metal over the coals, shaping it with hammers, working it with his strong arm. He gets hungry and loses his strength; he drinks no water and grows faint. The physical exhaustion of the craftsman making a god is Isaiah's ironic demolition: the human who fashions the divine grows hungry and faint in the process. The god-maker is more dependent than his creation — he needs food and water; his creation needs nothing because it does nothing. The contrast with YHWH who neither slumbers nor sleeps (Psalms 121:4) and whose creative word does not return to him empty (Isaiah 55:11) is total.
13: The carpenter measures the wood with a line and makes an outline with a stylus; he roughs it out with chisels and marks it with compasses. He shapes it in the form of a man, with the beauty of a man, to dwell in a shrine. The idol is formed in the image of a man — the inversion of Genesis 1:26-27 (God made humanity in his image). The idol-maker takes his own image and projects it onto wood; YHWH took his own image and projected it into humanity. The shrine-dwelling idol contrasts with YHWH who dwells in the temple made not by human hands (Acts 7:48, Isaiah 17:24) — and ultimately dwells in the body of Christ (John 2:21: the temple he had spoken of was his body) and in the Spirit-filled community (1 Corinthians 3:16).
14: He cut down cedars; he selected a cypress or an oak and let it grow among the trees of the forest; he planted a pine, and the rain made it grow. The irony extends to the lifecycle of the idol: the craftsman waits for a tree to grow, then cuts it down to make his god. YHWH planted Lebanon's cedars (Psalms 104:16); the idol-maker raids what YHWH made to manufacture an alternative. The time and cultivation invested in the future idol is the time YHWH invested in the creation that the idol-maker then exploits for his self-made worship.
15: Then a man uses it for fuel; he takes some of it and warms himself; he kindles a fire and bakes bread. He also makes a god and worships it; he makes an idol and bows down to it. The double use of the same wood — fuel and deity — is the central absurdity. Half burned, half worshiped. In the NT, Paul's version is the division of the human heart that serves two masters: no one can serve God and money (Matthew 6:24). The single piece of wood split between practical use and religious devotion is the image of the divided loyalty that Jesus names as the fundamental impossibility of authentic worship.
16: Half of it he burns in the fire; over half of it he eats meat; he roasts his food and is satisfied. Also he warms himself and says, Ah, I am warm; I see the fire. The three uses of the halved wood — warmth, cooking, and worship — are distributed across one object. The warmth-satisfaction (I am warm; I see the fire) is the physical contentment that immediately precedes the spiritual absurdity (v 17). The satiated body is the prelude to the worshiped stick. In the NT: those who say they are rich, have acquired wealth and do not need a thing, but are wretched, pitiful, poor, blind and naked (Revelation 3:17) — the body-satisfaction that masks the spiritual poverty of false worship.
17: The rest of it he makes into a god, his idol, and bows down to it and worships it. He prays to it and says, Save me! For you are my god. The climactic absurdity: Save me! prayed to a piece of wood. The prayer addressed to the manufactured half-log is the darkest inversion of prayer to YHWH. In the NT, the equivalent is calling on the name of the Lord for salvation (Romans 10:13, citing Joel 2:32) — but the name that saves is YHWH/Jesus, not manufactured things. The stick cannot save; the Servant who was given as a guilt offering (Isaiah 53:10) and who was himself made of wood (the cross) is the only one who can answer Save me!
18: They know nothing, they understand nothing; their eyes are plastered shut so they cannot see, and their minds are closed so they cannot understand. The spiritual blindness of the idol-worshiper is the counterpart of the spiritual sleep of Isaiah 29:10: YHWH seals the eyes of those who suppress the truth in wickedness (Romans 1:18). The plastered-shut eyes are the condition that only the Servant can reverse: I am sending you to open eyes that are blind (Acts 26:18, citing Isaiah 42:7). The idol-worshiper's eyes are closed in exact proportion to how open the servant of YHWH's eyes should be.
19: No one reflects on this; no one has the knowledge or understanding to say: Half of it I burned in the fire, and I also baked bread over its coals; I roasted meat and I ate it. Shall I make the rest of it into something detestable? Shall I bow down to a block of wood? The inability to reason is the spiritual condition of idolatry: no one stops to think about what they are doing. In the NT: the futility of their thinking (Romans 1:21) and the darkening of the mind (Ephesians 4:17-18) are the same failure of reflective reason — idolatry is not primarily intellectual error but the moral condition of suppressing the truth that YHWH is Creator and that the manufactured is not divine.
20: He feeds on ashes; a deluded mind has led him astray, and he cannot deliver himself or say, Is not this thing in my right hand a lie? The ashes-as-food is the culminating image of idolatry's bankruptcy: the nourishment idolatry promises turns out to be ash — substance that disintegrates. The deluded mind (lefat lev — the diverted heart) is the self-chosen captivity: the idol-worshiper cannot even deliver himself, let alone receive deliverance from his manufactured god. In the NT, the turning point is always repentance — the awakening from delusion. And coming to his senses (Luke 15:17: the prodigal son coming to himself) is the moment the ash-feeding ends and the return to the Father begins.
21: Remember these things, Jacob and Israel, for you are my servant. I have formed you, you are my servant; Israel, I will not forget you. The servant-identity is YHWH's counter-word to the idol-polemic: Jacob is not an idol-worshiper but YHWH's formed servant. The I will not forget you is the covenant-faithfulness promise that sustains the servant's identity through exile and crisis. In the NT: I will never leave you; never will I forsake you (Hebrews 13:5, citing Deuteronomy 31:6) and who shall separate us from the love of Christ? (Romans 8:35). The unforgetting of YHWH is the theological ground of the Servant's confidence — and the church's confidence through persecution.
22: I have swept away your transgressions like a cloud and your sins like the morning mist. Return to me, for I have redeemed you. The swept-away cloud-and-mist image is the most vivid OT picture of total forgiveness: the transgressions are as insubstantial as morning mist before the sun — they disappear completely at YHWH's act of redemption. The redemption precedes the return: I have redeemed you (past tense) therefore return to me (present command). This is the NT gospel structure: God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting people's sins against them (2 Corinthians 5:19) — the atonement precedes and grounds the call to reconciliation. The cloud-swept forgiveness is the act of propitiation (1 John 4:10) that makes the call to repentance meaningful.
23: Sing for joy, you heavens, for YHWH has done this; shout aloud, you earth beneath; burst into song, you mountains, you forests and all your trees; for YHWH has redeemed Jacob and displays his glory in Israel. The cosmic doxology at the act of redemption: creation sings at YHWH's purchase of Jacob. The mountains and forests that witnessed the idol-polemic (the craftsman cut down a cedar, burned half, worshiped the rest) now sing at the radical alternative — YHWH's redemption of his servant. In the NT: at the name of Jesus every knee will bow — in heaven and on earth and under the earth — and every tongue confess (Philippians 2:10-11). The creation-chorus of Isaiah 44:23 is the eschatological form of Phil 2's cosmic acknowledgment.
24: Thus says YHWH, your Redeemer, who formed you in the womb: I am YHWH, the Maker of all things, who stretches out the heavens by myself, who spreads out the earth — who was with me? The creation-claim — I am YHWH, the Maker of all things — is the direct antecedent of John 1:3 (all things were made through him, and without him nothing was made that has been made). YHWH's solo act of creation (who was with me?) is applied Christologically in Colossians 1:16 (all things have been created through him and for him) and Hebrews 1:2 (through whom he made the universe). The Father's creative agency and the Son's creative agency are identified in the NT as the single divine creative act — the Father creating through the Son who is YHWH.
25: Who foils the signs of false prophets and makes fools of diviners; who overthrows the learning of the wise and turns it into nonsense. The YHWH who confounds human wisdom-claims is the Christological type of 1 Corinthians 1:19-20 (I will destroy the wisdom of the wise and frustrate the intelligence of the intelligent — citing Isaiah 29:14). The cross is YHWH's supreme act of wisdom-confounding: the crucified Messiah is foolishness to Greeks (1 Corinthians 1:23) because it inverts every divinatory and philosophical calculus about what divine power looks like.
26: Who carries out the words of his servants and fulfills the predictions of his messengers; who says of Jerusalem, She shall be inhabited; of the cities of Judah, They shall be built; their ruins I will restore. The word-keeper who fulfills his prophets' predictions is the reliable God who confirms his own word. In the NT, the same faithfulness is applied to the fulfillment of messianic prophecy: not one jot or tittle will disappear from the law until everything is accomplished (Matthew 5:18). The YHWH who restores ruined Jerusalem is the same God who restores the ruined humanity — the ruins-I-will-restore is the gospel of resurrection, the re-inhabitation of what death had emptied.
27: Who says to the deep, Be dry, and I will dry up your rivers. The command to the watery deep is the Creator's sovereignty over the primordial chaos-waters: I say Be dry and they obey. The drying of rivers for the passage of YHWH's people is the Exodus type (Red Sea, Jordan crossing) applied now to the Cyrus-conquest (v 28) — YHWH will dry the Euphrates so Cyrus can conquer Babylon. In the NT, Jesus rebuking the storm (Mark 4:39: Be still!) and walking on water (Matthew 14:25-26) exercise the same Creator-sovereignty over the waters that YHWH claims here — the same voice that commanded the deep commands the storm.
28: Who says of Cyrus, He is my shepherd and will carry out all my purpose; he will say of Jerusalem, Let it be rebuilt, and of the temple, Let its foundations be laid. The naming of Cyrus by name 150 years before his birth is the most dramatic instance of predictive prophecy in Isaiah — and was historically specific: Cyrus of Persia issued the edict permitting the return (Ezra 1:1-4) precisely as this verse predicted. The shepherd title applied to Cyrus is the same title applied to YHWH in Ps 23 and to the Messiah in Ezekiel 34 and John 10 — but Cyrus is the type, not the fulfillment. Jesus is the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) who fulfills what Cyrus prefigures: the one who rebuilds the true temple (John 2:19-21), lays the true foundation (1 Corinthians 3:11), and carries out all of YHWH's purpose in the new creation's Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2).
